name: new_container_can_have_children_unconstrained
code:
proof fn phi_1_new_container_can_have_children_unconstrained(
    root_container: ContainerPtr,
    old_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    new_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    new_container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
)
    requires
        new_container_ensures(root_container, old_container_perms, new_container_perms, container_ptr, new_container_ptr),
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().can_have_children == true,
    ensures
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().can_have_children == true,
{
}
reason: new_container_ensures never constrains can_have_children — a freshly created container with empty children list could still be marked as able to have children, or not, with no policy enforced

name: parent_container_resources_unchanged_after_new
code:
proof fn phi_2_parent_container_resources_unchanged_after_new(
    root_container: ContainerPtr,
    old_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    new_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    new_container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    t: ThreadPtr,
)
    requires
        new_container_ensures(root_container, old_container_perms, new_container_perms, container_ptr, new_container_ptr),
        old_container_perms[container_ptr].value().owned_threads@.contains(t),
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().owned_threads@.contains(t),
    ensures
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().owned_threads@.contains(t),
{
}
reason: new_container_ensures doesn't constrain that resources moved to child are removed from parent — threads can appear in both parent and child owned_threads simultaneously

name: owned_procs_not_subset_of_subtree_procs
code:
proof fn phi_3_owned_procs_not_subset_of_subtree_procs(
    root_container: ContainerPtr,
    container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    c_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    p: ProcPtr,
)
    requires
        container_perms_wf(container_perms),
        container_tree_wf(root_container, container_perms),
        container_perms.dom().contains(c_ptr),
        container_perms[c_ptr].value().owned_procs@.contains(p),
        container_perms[c_ptr].value().root_process == Some(p),
    ensures
        container_perms[c_ptr].value().owned_procs@.contains(p),
{
}
reason: No spec relates root_process to owned_procs — root_process can be set to a ProcPtr that is also (or not) in owned_procs with no consistency check

name: new_container_owned_cpus_unconstrained
code:
proof fn phi_5_new_container_owned_cpus_unconstrained(
    root_container: ContainerPtr,
    old_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    new_container_perms: Map<ContainerPtr, PointsTo<Container>>,
    container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    new_container_ptr: ContainerPtr,
    cpu: usize,
)
    requires
        new_container_ensures(root_container, old_container_perms, new_container_perms, container_ptr, new_container_ptr),
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().owned_cpus.set@.contains(cpu),
        cpu >= NUM_CPUS,
    ensures
        new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().owned_cpus.set@.contains(cpu),
{
}
reason: new_container_ensures never constrains owned_cpus — a new container can claim CPUs with indices beyond NUM_CPUS, violating the ArraySet bounds
